source: .specs/agent-scenarios.md → Feature: Multiple sessions in same cwd
notes: DOCUMENTED TARGET ONLY — no recording (by_adapter.pi.applicable:false, driver_gap). Two concurrent pi REPL sessions launched in the SAME cwd. Each pi process mints its OWN session UUID and writes a distinct transcript under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl (session_id_assignment:false), so the two distinct session_ids are asserted via new_session/same_session_as relations, not literal ids. Pi's process scanner uses ExactName{pi} (pgrep -x), so a pre-session proc-<PID> row is born in ready BEFORE the transcript is discovered and never enters working; every phase therefore anchors on a WORKING transition so a ready-anchored new_session phase cannot greedily bind to that placeholder. Pi's DiscoverPID uses DiscoverPIDByCWD (cwd-only narrowing + a disambiguate fallback to highest PID) — UNLIKE codex's per-rollout-file-writer binding and claudecode's metadata-first lookup; two pi processes sharing one cwd both resolve to the same candidate set, so the daemon cannot cleanly bind each same-cwd session to its OWN PID by cwd alone. That observability seam is why this cell is recorded for the sibling adapters but documented-only here. Assertions mirror the codex/claudecode siblings: (1) session 1 does an independent working→ready turn; (2) a DISTINCT session (s2_turn1_start carries new_session:true) does its own working→ready turn; (3) session 1 is driven AGAIN while session 2 is alive (s1_turn2_*, pinned same_session_as s1_turn1_start), proving its binding stayed stable and never flapped to session 2. Phase order follows the would-be recipe: s1 turn → start_session → s2 turn → s1 turn again.

text: Session 1 first prompt drives ready → working (anchors session 1's transcript id; the pre-session proc-<PID> row and the transcript's born-ready transition are both skipped by anchoring on working)
Session 1 first turn completes: working → ready

Session 2 appears in the SAME cwd as a DISTINCT transcript id and drives its first turn ready → working — independent of session 1 (new_session proves it is not session 1; anchoring on working skips pi's born-ready proc-<PID> placeholder)
Session 2 first turn completes: working → ready
Session 1 is driven AGAIN while session 2 is still alive — a working transition on session 1's original transcript id proves the binding stayed stable and did not flap to session 2
Session 1 second turn completes: working → ready
